title: Early Convolutions, Late Attention
concept: Placing convolutional layers early in the network extracts low-level features and stabilizes optimization, while later transformer layers model global semantics.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In a backbone hybrid model, what serves as the patch tokens?

options.text: The spatial grid cells of the backbone's output feature map.
The raw pixels of the input image sliced by a grid.
The text embeddings from an NLP head.

exercises.explain: Correct! The cells of the CNN's feature map are treated directly as tokens.

cards.title: The division of labor
cards.body: When a network processes an image, it has two jobs: look closely at textures, and look broadly at layout. Putting convolutions at the beginning (early) and transformers at the end (late) is a highly optimized division of labor. Let's see why.

cards.title: Early Conv, Late Attention
cards.body: This design pattern restricts convolutions to early layers (which have small receptive fields and excel at edge detection) and reserves self-attention for late layers (where features are abstract and global relationships matter). This avoids running expensive attention on large, raw spatial resolutions.

cards.title: A microscope and a telescope
cards.body: Imagine analyzing a painting. You start by using a microscope (convolutions) to inspect the pigment and weave of the canvas locally. Then, you step back and use a telescope (attention) to map how the characters are arranged across the gallery. Doing it in reverse would make no sense!

cards.title: ViT-CoAtNet
cards.body: CoAtNet (Convolution and Attention Network) stacks convolutional blocks in stages 1 and 2 to reduce resolution, then switches to self-attention blocks in stages 3 and 4. This model achieved top ImageNet accuracy with fast training.

cards.title: Not just for accuracy
cards.body: You might think this split is only to squeeze out 1% more accuracy. In fact, it also solves a **major training issue**: pure ViT optimization is highly sensitive to learning rate and weight decay. Adding early convs makes the optimization curve smooth and easy to train.

left.text:
Input: High Resolution
Operation: Convolutions
Extracts: Edges, colors, textures

right.text:
Input: Low Resolution (downsampled)
Operation: Self-Attention
Extracts: Semantic objects, global context

exercises.prompt: Why are convolutional layers placed in the early stages of a hybrid network?
exercises.explain: Convolutions handle high-resolution inputs very efficiently, extracting local textures and stabilizing gradient flow.

options.text: To capture high-resolution local features efficiently and stabilize early training
To classification label the tokens in stage 1
To calculate global context weights before seeing edges
To prevent the model from using GPUs

exercises.prompt: True or false: Running self-attention on the raw high-resolution input image is computationally cheaper than running it on a downsampled feature map.
exercises.explain: False. High-resolution attention creates long sequences, which scale quadratically and require far more compute than downsampled inputs.
Downsampled feature maps have fewer spatial dimensions, keeping the transformer sequence length short and efficient.
exercises.sentence: By placing self-attention late in the network, we apply global modeling to ___ feature maps.

exercises.prompt: Which architecture layout represents the 'Early Convolutions, Late Attention' design pattern?
exercises.explain: Correct! Running Conv2D and pooling layers first downsamples the spatial grid before routing tokens through the TransformerEncoder.

options.text: Conv2D -> Conv2D -> MaxPool -> TransformerEncoder -> TransformerEncoder -> MLP

exercises.prompt: Why does adding early convolutions make training less sensitive to hyperparameters?

options.text: Because convolutions provide a consistent, structured starting point for gradient optimization, preventing the chaotic gradient updates common in early ViT layers.
Because convolutions completely remove the loss function.
Because convolutions force the learning rate to remain constant.
Because convolutions make position embeddings redundant.

exercises.explain: Splendid! Early convolutions inject structure into the pipeline, smoothing out the loss landscape for easier training. Next: lightweight edge-inference hybrids like LeViT and ConViT!
